text: ## Sprint 12 (iOS) — Release readiness - Target: ship `v2.6.0` on Feb 28, 2026. - Blocker: Crash in `SettingsViewController` when toggling “Use Cellular Data”. Repro: iPhone 15 Pro simulator, iOS 17.3, clean install. - Fix: guard `selectedPlan` nil path; add unit test `testCellularToggleDoesNotCrash()`. - QA checklist: run regression on onboarding, login, and settings; confirm no crash in `SettingsViewController`. - Build info: Xcode 15.3, Swift 5.9. Decision log: - We will keep the feature flag `cellular_data_toggle` enabled for internal builds only until after `v2.6.0` ships.

## Backend incident note — 2026-02-14 At 09:12 UTC, the `/v1/payments/charge` endpoint began returning elevated `HTTP 502` responses. Primary symptom: gateway timeouts from `nginx` when upstream latency exceeded 30s. We confirmed the root cause was a saturated connection pool on the payments service after a deploy. Mitigation steps completed: - Rolled back to `payments-service@3d9a2c1` at 09:26 UTC. - Increased pool size from 20 to 60 and redeployed at 09:41 UTC. Validation: - Error rate returned to baseline by 09:48 UTC. - Follow-up: add alert for `HTTP 502` on `/v1/payments/charge` and cap upstream timeout to 15s.

## Analytics spec — Events to add We are adding 3 new events for the checkout experiment `exp_checkout_layout_7`. 1) `checkout_viewed` - Fire on first render of the checkout screen. - Properties: `cart_id`, `user_id`, `variant`. 2) `checkout_address_saved` - Fire after user taps Save and the API returns 200. - Properties: `address_id`, `is_default`, `variant`. 3) `checkout_payment_submitted` - Fire after payment submit button tap. - Properties: `payment_method`, `amount_cents`, `variant`. Implementation details: - Use `Segment` iOS SDK v4. - Ensure `variant` matches the experiment assignment at time of event. - QA: validate events in the Segment debugger and confirm `exp_checkout_layout_7` is present.

## DB migration log Migration `20260210_add_user_timezone` executed in production on 2026-02-10 at 22:05 UTC. Change: - Added nullable column `user_profile.timezone` (VARCHAR(64)). - Backfilled for 94% of users using last known device offset; remaining left NULL. Rollback plan: - If needed, run `DROP COLUMN user_profile.timezone` (requires maintenance window). Verification: - Query: `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM user_profile WHERE timezone IS NULL;` returned 18,204 at 22:21 UTC. - Application version deployed with this migration: `api@v1.42.3`. Follow-ups: - Add validation to reject invalid IANA timezone strings. - Update admin UI to display `user_profile.timezone`.

## Security patch note We rotated credentials for the `github-actions` service account on 2026-01-30. Reason: - The previous token was accidentally printed in CI logs for 38 seconds during a failed deploy job. Actions taken: - Revoked the old token immediately. - Created a new token and stored it in `CI_GITHUB_TOKEN` secret. - Updated workflows to avoid echoing environment variables. Verification: - Confirmed checkout and release jobs can still publish artifacts. - Confirmed no access using the revoked token. Reminder: - Do not reference `CI_GITHUB_TOKEN` in debug output. - All changes were merged via PR #418 and the secret rotation was documented in the runbook.

## Mobile release notes — Android Release `Android 3.18.1` went to 10% staged rollout on 2026-02-05. Fixes included: - Resolved crash when opening push notifications with deep link `app://orders/receipt`. - Updated network stack to handle TLS handshake failures more gracefully. Operational details: - Play Console track: production. - Monitoring: watch `Crashlytics` for spikes in `NullPointerException` in `ReceiptActivity`. Results after 24 hours: - Crash-free users improved from 99.10% to 99.62%. - No regressions reported in login or checkout. Next: - If metrics stay stable, increase rollout to 50% on 2026-02-07.

## Vendor contract summary We renewed the CDN contract with `FastEdge CDN` on 2026-02-01. Key terms: - Annual cost: $48,000 billed monthly. - Included bandwidth: 120 TB/month. - Overage: $0.04 per GB. - SLA: 99.9% monthly uptime. Administrative details: - Vendor ID in finance system: `VND-20491`. - Contract period: 2026-02-01 through 2027-01-31. Implementation note: - Origin shield enabled for `static.example.com`. - Any changes to `static.example.com` caching rules must be reviewed by Ops before rollout. Next review: - Re-evaluate pricing at 6 months based on traffic growth.

## Hardware inventory — DevOps Received and racked new build server `macmini-ci-07` on 2026-02-12. Specs: - Apple Mac mini (M2 Pro), 32GB RAM, 1TB SSD. - Installed macOS 14.3 and Xcode 15.3. Configuration: - Added to Jenkins node list as label `macos-arm64`. - Provisioned keychain for code signing with team cert `iOS Distribution: Collab Inc (9A1B2C3D4E)`. Validation: - Ran pipeline “ios-nightly” successfully at 03:18 UTC. - Confirmed it can build and archive `CollabApp` without manual prompts. Notes: - Keep the hostname `macmini-ci-07` reserved in DNS; do not reuse for other machines.

## Project: Collab iOS On 2026-01-14, we shipped build `2.3.0 (410)` of Collab iOS to TestFlight. The release notes mention the new “Pinned Threads” UI and a fix for a crash when opening a deep link from notifications. The rollout was limited to the internal testers group only; no external testing was enabled. The build was created from branch `release/2.3.0` and tagged in git as `ios-2.3.0-410`. QA sign-off was recorded in the #mobile-release channel after a 30-minute sanity pass on iPhone 15 Pro (iOS 17.2). We also bumped the minimum supported iOS version to 16.0 in this build and verified that the app launches cleanly on iPhone SE (2nd gen).

## Backend: Billing Service We rotated the production Stripe webhook signing secret on 2025-12-03 due to a suspected leak in a vendor log export. The old secret was revoked the same day, and the new secret was stored in AWS Secrets Manager under `billing/stripe/webhook_secret`. We updated the verifier to accept only the new secret and deployed to production at 19:20 UTC. After deployment, we replayed 25 webhook events from Stripe’s dashboard to confirm signature validation and idempotency behavior. The incident ticket is `INC-1847` and includes timestamps, affected systems, and the remediation checklist. Monitoring showed no increase in failed webhook deliveries after the change. The on-call engineer for the rotation was Dana Kim.

## Data Warehouse Notes On 2026-02-01, we created a new BigQuery table for daily app events: `analytics.events_daily_v2`. It replaces `analytics.events_daily` and adds a required `device_timezone` column plus a new partitioning strategy by `event_date`. The backfill job ran for 2025-10-01 through 2026-01-31 and completed in 2h 18m. We validated row counts per day against the old table and confirmed the delta stayed under 0.3% after excluding late-arriving events. The scheduled query owner is `data-platform@collab.com`, and the schedule is set to run at 03:10 UTC every day. The change request was approved in PR `#7921`.

## Mobile CI/CD The iOS pipeline was updated on 2025-11-18 to use Xcode `15.1` on the hosted macOS runners. The workflow file `/.github/workflows/ios.yml` now pins the runner image and installs dependencies via `bundle exec fastlane ios build`. We also enabled caching for Swift Package Manager at `~/Library/Caches/org.swift.swiftpm` to reduce cold-start times. The first successful build using the new setup was run `#1289` and finished in 12m 44s (previous median was 19m). We documented the migration in the internal wiki under “CI: Xcode 15.1 rollout” and noted that simulator tests remain disabled on PRs for now. The change was merged by Alex Rivera.

## Security Patch Log We patched OpenSSL in the Ubuntu base image for our API container on 2026-01-28. The Docker image tag is `api-base:2026.01.28` and it upgrades OpenSSL to `3.0.13-0ubuntu3.2`. After rebuilding, we scanned the image and confirmed that CVE `CVE-2024-5535` no longer appears in the report. The deployment to production happened during the 02:00–02:30 UTC window with no downtime. We updated the runtime manifests in `k8s/prod/api-deployment.yaml` to reference the new base tag and verified pods rolled successfully across both clusters. The change log entry is filed under `SECCHANGE-332` with links to the scan artifacts.

## Customer Support Playbook For Acme Corp, we set up a dedicated SSO configuration on 2025-09-09. The IdP metadata URL is stored as `https://idp.acme.com/metadata` and the tenant identifier is `acme-prod`. We validated the SAML assertion mapping for email, first_name, and last_name, and confirmed that Just-In-Time provisioning creates users with the default role `member`. The configuration was enabled in production at 16:45 UTC and tested by logging in with user `it.admin@acme.com`. We captured screenshots of the successful login flow and attached them to ticket `SUP-22914`. Acme requested a 12-hour session duration; we implemented that and documented it in the tenant notes.

## Observability We added a new Grafana dashboard on 2026-02-10 named `API Latency - p95 by Route`. It queries Prometheus metric `http_server_requests_seconds_bucket` and filters for service label `api-gateway`. The dashboard UID is `kQ9d2LmVz` and it includes panels for p50, p95, and p99, plus an error-rate overlay. Alerting was configured to page on-call if p95 exceeds 900ms for 10 minutes on `/v1/messages/send`. We verified the alert rule by temporarily introducing 1.2s latency in the staging environment and watching the state transition to Firing. The dashboard is linked in the runbook under “Performance triage.”

## Payments Release Notes On 2025-10-22, we enabled Apple Pay for the iOS app in production. The merchant identifier is `merchant.com.collab.payments`, and the entitlement was added to the main app target only (not the share extension). We also updated the server to accept the new token type and confirmed successful charges using sandbox test card flows before flipping the feature flag. The rollout was controlled via LaunchDarkly flag `applepay_enabled` set to 10% for the first hour, then 100% after no errors appeared in Sentry. The implementation was merged in PR `#6810` and referenced in the release checklist for version 2.2.1. Finance confirmed settlement in the Stripe dashboard the next morning.

## Project: Collab iOS We migrated the caching layer from `NSCache` to a small LRU wrapper to reduce memory churn during long scrolling sessions. The LRU capacity is capped at 250 items, and eviction is triggered on insert when full. Profiling was done on iPhone 13 (iOS 17.2) using Instruments Allocations. Key findings: peak resident size dropped from 612MB to 438MB after the change, and the number of transient `UIImage` allocations decreased by ~28%. The largest remaining spikes correlate with image decode on the main thread, not cache growth. Action items: - keep `ImageLRUCache` behind the same protocol as before - add a guardrail metric in CI to flag regressions above 500MB

peak resident size dropped from 612MB to 438MB

text: ## Backend notes: Auth service rollout On January 12, 2026, we enabled the new token issuer for 25% of traffic in production. Requests for `/v2/session` now return JWTs signed with `kid=auth-2026-01`. We verified signature validation in the API gateway and confirmed old tokens remain valid until their natural expiry. Incident log: between 14:05–14:22 UTC we saw a 1.8% increase in 401 responses from Android clients on version 4.9.1. Root cause was a clock-skew tolerance misconfiguration (set to 0s instead of 60s). After adjusting the tolerance, 401s returned to baseline. Follow-up: - keep dual signing active for 14 days - publish the new public keys to the JWKS endpoint

## Data pipeline: nightly export The nightly export job `orders_export_v3` was moved from cron to Airflow DAG `exports.orders.v3` on February 3, 2026. The schedule is `0 2 * * *` (02:00 UTC) and writes partitioned Parquet to `s3://acme-analytics/exports/orders/v3/`. Validation results from the first full run: - rows exported: 18,442,109 - output size: 96.3GB - checksum match rate vs source DB: 100% We also confirmed that the DAG uses the new IAM role `airflow-exports-prod` and that retries are capped at 2 with exponential backoff. TODO: - add a data quality check for duplicate `order_id` - document the backfill procedure for missed days

## Release checklist: Android app We shipped Android 5.2.0 to 100% on February 10, 2026. The Play Console track was Production, and the release name was `5.2.0 (502000)`. Primary changes included enabling the new OkHttp interceptor for request signing and updating the target SDK to 34. Post-release monitoring: - crash-free users: 99.62% - top crash signature: `NullPointerException` in `CheckoutViewModel.kt:214` - affected devices: mostly Samsung Galaxy S21 We created hotfix branch `hotfix/5.2.1-signing` and reverted the interceptor change for checkout only. The ANR rate remained stable at 0.12%. Next steps: - add a nullability guard around `selectedShippingMethod` - backport the fix to the release branch

## Infrastructure: Redis upgrade We upgraded the primary Redis cluster from 6.2.6 to 7.0.12 on January 29, 2026. The change was performed via rolling restart across 3 nodes, with replicas promoted sequentially to avoid downtime. Client libraries were already compatible, so no app deploy was required. Metrics: - p99 GET latency improved from 4.8ms to 3.1ms - memory fragmentation ratio dropped from 1.72 to 1.29 - replication lag stayed under 250ms during the rollout Config notes: - `maxmemory-policy` remains `allkeys-lru` - `timeout` set to 300 We validated persistence by forcing a snapshot and confirming keys after restart.
required_spans: p99 GET latency improved from 4.8ms to 3.1ms

text: ## Security audit: dependency pinning During the SCA scan on February 6, 2026, we identified a vulnerable transitive dependency in `org.apache.logging.log4j:log4j-core`. The impacted version was 2.14.1, pulled in by an older internal tool. We remediated by pinning `log4j-core` to 2.17.2 and adding an explicit exclusion for the older artifact. Verification: - build green on CI job `java-server-main` - no remaining findings tagged `CVE-2021-44228` - smoke test passed on staging environment `stg-eu-1` Notes: - keep the lockfile committed - schedule a follow-up scan after merging to `main`

## Payments: Stripe webhooks We registered a new webhook endpoint on February 14, 2026 for handling subscription lifecycle events. The configured URL is `https://api.acme.com/webhooks/stripe/v1`, and it listens for `invoice.payment_succeeded` and `customer.subscription.deleted`. The signing secret was stored in Vault under `secret/payments/stripe/webhook_secret`. Test run: - replayed 20 historical events from the Stripe dashboard - verified signature validation with the `Stripe-Signature` header - confirmed idempotency by keying on `event.id` Operational note: the endpoint enforces a 5-second timeout and returns HTTP 200 only after the event is persisted. Follow-up: - add alerting when 5xx exceeds 0.5% in 10 minutes

## Observability: tracing rollout We enabled distributed tracing for the `checkout-service` on January 18, 2026 using OpenTelemetry SDK 1.32.0. Traces are exported to Tempo via OTLP gRPC endpoint `tempo.prod.local:4317`. We instrumented HTTP client calls and Postgres queries, and added a span attribute for `cart_id`. Results from the first day: - sampling rate set to 0.15 - average trace size: 7.4KB - top slow span: `POST /checkout/confirm` with p95 at 1.9s We also updated Grafana dashboards to link from error logs to trace IDs and verified correlation in production. Next step: add baggage propagation to the mobile clients.

## API client update (iOS) On 2026-01-08, I updated the iOS networking layer to add a new endpoint and aligned decoding with the backend contract. The new call is `GET /v1/users/me` and it returns `UserProfileDTO` with fields `id`, `email`, and `createdAt` (ISO-8601). I also renamed the old method from `fetchCurrentUser()` to `getCurrentUserProfile()` to match our naming convention. The change was reviewed by Priya and merged into `main` the same day. A follow-up note: the backend requires header `X-App-Version` and rejects requests without it.

## Build + test baseline On 2025-12-03, I verified the project builds cleanly on macOS 14.2 with Xcode 15.1. The exact command used was `xcodebuild -scheme Collab -destination 'platform=iOS Simulator,name=iPhone 15' test`. The run finished in 6m 12s and produced an `.xcresult` bundle named `CollabTests_2025-12-03.xcresult`. Two tests were flaky earlier in the week, but on this run they passed consistently: `MemoryStoreTests.testPersistenceRoundTrip` and `SyncEngineTests.testMergeConflictResolution`. I saved the console output in our internal build logs and noted that caching was disabled to ensure a clean baseline.

## Database migration record On 2025-11-19, I added a schema migration for the local SQLite store to support tagging memories. The migration increments the schema from version 7 to version 8 and creates a new table `memory_tags` with columns `memory_id` (TEXT) and `tag` (TEXT). I also added an index `idx_memory_tags_memory_id` to speed up lookups during sync. This was tested by upgrading an existing install with ~4,200 rows; the migration completed in under 200ms on an iPhone 13 simulator. I documented the rollback plan: if migration fails, we delete the store file and rehydrate from the server.

## Incident note: auth regression On 2026-02-01, I investigated a login failure affecting iOS builds after the token refresh change. The root cause was a missing query parameter in the refresh request: it must include `grant_type=refresh_token` or the auth server returns HTTP 400. The error signature in logs was `invalid_grant`, and it reproduced consistently on build `1.9.4 (204)`. I patched the request builder and confirmed the fix by replaying the same refresh flow in Charles; the server then returned 200 with a new access token. I also added a small unit test around refresh request encoding to prevent regression.

## Release checklist (completed) For the 2025-10-14 release, I completed the iOS release checklist and recorded the exact artifacts. The version shipped to TestFlight was `2.0.0 (310)`, and the build was signed with the distribution profile `Collab iOS Distribution`. The release notes included the line “Improved offline memory search” and we verified the feature on device in airplane mode. I confirmed App Store Connect processing succeeded at 17:42 PT and that the submission used the `CollabProd` configuration. The tag `ios-2.0.0` was created in git immediately after the upload.

## Performance measurement On 2025-09-27, I ran a profiling session to measure the cold-start time of the app on an iPhone 14 Pro simulator. Using Instruments with the Time Profiler template, the median cold start was 1.84s across 10 launches. The heaviest single function on the main thread was `MemoryIndex.buildTrie()`, accounting for ~220ms in the slowest launch. After toggling the feature flag `useIncrementalIndexing=true`, the median improved to 1.31s and the same function dropped below 90ms. I saved the trace file as `ColdStart_2025-09-27.trace` and attached it to the performance ticket PERF-118.

### Local dev: run iOS unit tests 1. Open Xcode and select the `Collab` scheme. 2. Set destination to `iPhone 15` simulator. 3. Clean build folder: Product → Clean Build Folder. 4. Run tests with Cmd+U. 5. If it hangs, reset DerivedData, then rerun. Notes: - Prefer running a focused test plan first, then the full suite. - If a single test fails only on CI, compare the simulator OS version and timezone. - Keep logs: open the Report navigator and export the `.xcresult`. If you need to rerun just one test file, use the Test navigator to select it and run that subset. This is the standard workflow we follow before pushing to main.

### Add a new Swift file to the app target Procedure: 1. In Xcode, right-click the group where it belongs (usually `Sources/App`). 2. Choose New File… → Swift File. 3. Name it using UpperCamelCase. 4. In the target membership panel, ensure `Collab` is checked. 5. Build once to confirm the file is compiled and no lint rules fail. Conventions: - Put models in `Sources/Domain` and view code in `Sources/UI`. - Keep the new file’s public API small; add internal helpers below. If the file doesn’t show up in the build, it’s almost always because target membership wasn’t enabled. Reopen the file inspector and fix it there.

### Rotate API keys used by local runs Steps: 1. Open `.env.local` in the repo root. 2. Replace the existing value for `API_KEY`. 3. In Keychain Access, delete the old saved credential if one exists. 4. Quit and relaunch the app so the new value is loaded. Verification: - Trigger any endpoint call and confirm the response is 200. - If you see 401, double-check there are no trailing spaces in `.env.local`. Important: - Never commit `.env.local`. - If you need to share a key, use the team password manager and include expiry date. This is the repeatable rotation routine we use whenever credentials are suspected to be leaked.

### Release checklist for TestFlight Process: 1. Bump the build number in the project settings. 2. Update release notes in `CHANGELOG.md`. 3. Archive via Product → Archive. 4. In the Organizer, Validate App. 5. Distribute App → App Store Connect → Upload. After upload: - In App Store Connect, select the new build and attach release notes. - Submit for beta review if required. Troubleshooting: - If Validate App fails on entitlements, recheck the signing settings and provisioning profile. - If upload is slow, retry from a stable network; the archive is cached. We do not skip validation; it catches signing issues before the build hits testers.

### Reproduce and file a crash with a symbolicated stack Steps: 1. Run the app on a simulator with Debug configuration. 2. Perform the exact interaction sequence until it crashes. 3. Open the Debug navigator and copy the exception details. 4. Export the crash log from Devices and Simulators. 5. Symbolicate using the matching dSYM from the archived build. Filing: - Create a ticket with title, repro steps, expected vs actual. - Attach the symbolicated stack trace and the raw crash log. If symbolication fails, it’s usually a dSYM mismatch—confirm the build number aligns with the crash log’s version. This workflow ensures engineering can act immediately without asking for more info.

### Set up a fresh clone and run the app Checklist: 1. Clone the repo and open the workspace file. 2. Run `bundle install` for Ruby tooling. 3. Run `pod install` (if the project uses CocoaPods). 4. Open the `.xcworkspace` and select the main scheme. 5. Build and run on a simulator. Common fixes: - If pods are missing, delete `Pods/` and rerun `pod install`. - If signing blocks the run, switch to Automatic Signing and select your team. Confirm success by seeing the home screen load and a network request complete. This is the standard onboarding procedure we follow for any new machine.

### Make a small schema change and migrate local data Procedure: 1. Update the model definition in the persistence layer. 2. Add a new migration step with an incremented version. 3. Write a transform for renamed or split fields. 4. Run the app once to trigger the migration. 5. Verify by inspecting the updated records in the debug UI. Rollback plan: - If data looks wrong, delete the local store file and rerun to regenerate. Notes: - Keep migrations additive when possible. - Always test upgrading from the previous released version by installing that build first, then updating. This repeatable migration routine prevents silent data loss when the schema evolves.

### Configure a new CI job for pull requests Steps: 1. Duplicate the existing PR pipeline config. 2. Update the job name and set the trigger to PR events. 3. Add a cache key for dependencies to speed builds. 4. Run unit tests and upload artifacts on failure. Validation: - Open a draft PR and confirm the job starts. - Ensure the job posts status checks back to the PR. Hard rules: - The job must fail fast on lint errors. - Artifacts must include the test report bundle. When this is done correctly, reviewers get immediate feedback without pulling the branch locally, and flaky failures have logs attached for investigation.

### Release checklist (iOS app) When cutting a new build, do this in order: 1. Bump version and build number in Xcode. 2. Run unit tests and UI smoke. 3. Archive with the Release configuration. 4. Upload to TestFlight and wait for processing. 5. Tag the commit and write release notes. Always verify the API base URL is pointing to prod before archiving. After upload, confirm the build appears under the correct app and internal group. If any tests fail, stop and fix before archiving; don’t “ship and pray.” Command notes: - Use `xcodebuild -scheme App -configuration Release test` for CI parity. - Then `xcodebuild -scheme App -configuration Release archive`.
required_spans: Bump version and build number in Xcode.

text: ### On-call triage steps (backend) If an alert fires for elevated 5xx, follow this runbook: 1. Check the dashboard for error rate and latency. 2. Compare against deploy timestamps. 3. Inspect logs for the top exception signature. 4. If it correlates with the latest deploy, roll back. 5. If not, rate-limit or shed load, then investigate deeper. Start with the user-impacting path; don’t chase minor warnings. Capture a quick snapshot of the graphs before making changes so you can explain what improved. If you roll back, announce in the incident channel, then open a postmortem doc. Concrete actions: - `kubectl -n prod rollout undo deploy/api` - `kubectl -n prod logs deploy/api --since=10m`
required_spans: If it correlates with the latest deploy, roll back.
Capture a quick snapshot of the graphs

text: ### Local dev environment setup (new laptop) Do this once after cloning the repo: 1. Install Xcode and accept the license. 2. Install Homebrew packages (mint, swiftlint, xcbeautify). 3. Set up Ruby via rbenv for fastlane. 4. Fetch secrets from 1Password. 5. Generate the `.env` file and confirm the app boots. Order matters: tools before gems, secrets before running the app. If you skip the secrets step, builds succeed but runtime calls fail. Commands: - `brew bundle` - `bundle install` - `fastlane ios bootstrap` Final verification: run the simulator and sign in using a test account.

### Bug reproduction protocol When QA reports a crash, reproduce it consistently before coding: 1. Ask for exact app version, device, OS, and locale. 2. Clear app state, then follow the steps exactly as written. 3. If it reproduces, record screen + logs. 4. If it doesn’t, try with a fresh install and airplane-mode toggles. 5. Create a minimal repro case and attach artifacts to the ticket. Always timebox: spend 20 minutes max before escalating questions back to QA. The goal is a deterministic sequence, not a theory. Artifacts to capture: - `Console.app` filtered by process - A short screen recording Ticket must include: **Expected vs actual**, plus your minimal steps.
required_spans: Clear app state, then follow the steps exactly as written.

text: ### Weekly dependency update process Every Monday, update dependencies without breaking main: 1. Create a branch named `deps/week-YYMMDD`. 2. Update Swift packages and CocoaPods. 3. Run lint and the full unit test suite. 4. If tests pass, open a PR with a clear changelog. 5. If tests fail, revert the smallest offending bump first. Keep changes mechanical: don’t refactor while updating deps. If a major version lands, split it into a separate PR with migration notes. Commands I use: - `bundle exec pod update` - `swift package update` PR checklist: link release notes for any major bumps and include before/after build times.
required_spans: Keep changes mechanical: don’t refactor while updating deps.

text: ### Database migration steps (production) When shipping a schema change, use this exact sequence: 1. Write the migration and test it on a staging snapshot. 2. Deploy code that is backward compatible first. 3. Run the migration during low-traffic hours. 4. Monitor error rate, slow queries, and replication lag. 5. After confidence, deploy the code that depends on the new schema. Never combine destructive column drops with the first deploy; do them in a later cleanup migration. Operational commands: - `rails db:migrate` - `rails db:rollback STEP=1` If replication lag spikes, pause writes or revert quickly.
required_spans: Deploy code that is backward compatible first.
Run the migration during low-traffic hours.

text: ### PR review routine (team standard) Before approving any PR, follow this checklist: 1. Read the description and verify the ticket link. 2. Scan the diff for risky areas (auth, payments, migrations). 3. Pull the branch and run tests locally. 4. Check for logging/metrics, and ensure errors are actionable. 5. Leave comments as questions or suggestions, then request changes or approve. Avoid bikeshedding: focus on correctness, security, and maintainability. If you suggest refactors, propose a follow-up ticket. Local commands: - `git fetch origin pull/123/head:pr-123` - `make test` Always confirm that new code has coverage or a clear reason why not.
required_spans: Pull the branch and run tests locally.
Avoid bikeshedding: focus on correctness, security, and maintainability.

text: ### Incident postmortem workflow After resolving an incident, write a postmortem within 48 hours: 1. Create the doc using the team template. 2. Fill in timeline with exact timestamps and decisions. 3. Identify root cause and contributing factors. 4. List action items with owners and due dates. 5. Review in the next engineering meeting. Keep it blameless and specific. Include what detection missed and what would have reduced time-to-mitigate. Template sections to include verbatim: - “What happened?” - “Why it happened” - “How we fixed it” Final step: post the link in the incident channel and pin it.
required_spans: Fill in timeline with exact timestamps and decisions.
List action items with owners and due dates.

text: ### Release checklist (iOS app) When cutting a release, follow this exact order: 1) Update `CHANGELOG.md` with the version header and bullet list. 2) Bump version + build in Xcode target settings. 3) Run `xcodebuild test -scheme App -destination "platform=iOS Simulator,name=iPhone 15"`. 4) Archive in Xcode (Product → Archive), then validate. 5) Tag the commit as `vX.Y.Z` and push tags. 6) Upload to TestFlight, then add release notes. If tests fail, stop and fix before archiving. If validation warns about icons or entitlements, resolve warnings and re-archive. Don’t change the order; it prevents mismatched build numbers and missing notes.

### Adding a new API endpoint (Swift) Use this procedure whenever you introduce a new endpoint: 1) Create a request model (Codable) named `XRequest`. 2) Create a response model (Codable) named `XResponse`. 3) Add an enum case in `APIEndpoint` with the path and HTTP method. 4) Implement the client method on `APIClient` with signature `func x(_ request: XRequest) async throws -> XResponse`. 5) Add a unit test that stubs the URL, asserts method/path, and decodes sample JSON. Before merging, confirm the method uses the shared JSON encoder/decoder and that the test fails if the path changes. Avoid ad-hoc URLSession code in feature modules.
required_spans: Add a unit test that stubs the URL

text: ### Restoring a broken dev environment If the app suddenly won’t build on your machine, do this sequence: 1) Quit Xcode. 2) Delete Derived Data: `rm -rf ~/Library/Developer/Xcode/DerivedData`. 3) Reset SPM caches in Xcode (File → Packages → Reset Package Caches). 4) Reopen the `.xcworkspace` (not the `.xcodeproj`). 5) Clean build folder (Shift+Cmd+K), then build. If you still see linker errors, verify the selected scheme matches the workspace and that the active configuration is Debug. Don’t skip step 2; it clears stale module artifacts that keep resurfacing after package updates.

### Creating a feature flag Standard flow for adding a feature flag: 1) Add the key to `FeatureFlagKey` enum. 2) Provide a default in `FeatureFlagDefaults`. 3) Expose a computed property on `FeatureFlags` (e.g., `var isNewPaywallEnabled: Bool`). 4) Gate UI and behavior behind the computed property. 5) Add a simple test verifying the default value. For staging overrides, only use the remote config provider; do not hardcode “true” in the feature module. When the feature ships permanently, remove the flag and delete the key, defaults, and property together in the same PR.
required_spans: Gate UI and behavior behind the computed property.

text: ### Handling secrets for local runs Procedure for adding a new secret used by the app: 1) Add the entry to `Secrets.template.xcconfig` with an obvious placeholder. 2) Add the real value to `Secrets.xcconfig` locally (never commit). 3) Ensure `Secrets.xcconfig` is listed in `.gitignore`. 4) Reference the value via build settings (e.g., `$(API_KEY)`) and pass into runtime config. Before opening a PR, search for the literal value to confirm it’s not in source, tests, or logs. If CI needs it, add it to the CI secret store and update the pipeline variable mapping, not the repo.
required_spans: Reference the value via build settings

text: ### Responding to a crash report When you get a new crash in Crashlytics, follow this workflow: 1) Open the crash, note the app version and the top frame. 2) Reproduce locally on the same OS version if possible. 3) Add a failing test that covers the minimal reproduction. 4) Fix the root cause and re-run the specific test. 5) Add a small breadcrumb log only if it would have made diagnosis faster. 6) Merge, then verify the fix in the next build’s crash-free rate. Don’t “fix” by adding broad try/catch without understanding why it crashed; that hides regressions and makes future triage slower.
required_spans: Add a failing test that covers the minimal reproduction.
Fix the root cause and re-run the specific test.
verify the fix in the next build’s crash-free rate

text: ### Adding a new screen (SwiftUI) Use this recipe for new SwiftUI screens: 1) Create `XView` and keep it purely declarative. 2) Create `XViewModel` with `@MainActor` and expose `@Published` state. 3) Inject dependencies via initializer (no singletons). 4) In previews, use `XViewModel.stub()` to render key states. 5) Add a navigation entry point and a snapshot/unit test if applicable. If the screen needs async loading, start it from `.task { await viewModel.onAppear() }` and ensure cancellation is handled. Keep formatting logic out of the view; put it on the view model so it’s testable.

### Database migration steps When changing persisted models, perform migrations like this: 1) Increment the schema version constant. 2) Add a new migration block named `migrateToV<N>()`. 3) In the migration, read old records, transform fields, then write new records. 4) Add a verification step that counts records before/after and asserts invariants. 5) Run the migration on a copy of a real production export. Never mutate the live file during development; always work on a duplicate so you can retry quickly. Once verified, wire the migration into app startup before the first database access to avoid mixed-version reads.
required_spans: Add a new migration block named `migrateToV<N>()`.
wire the migration into app startup

text: ### iOS release workflow (App Store) When cutting a release, start by syncing `main` locally and pulling tags. Create a branch named `release/<version>` and bump the version + build number in Xcode. Run unit tests, then archive via Xcode using the Release scheme. Upload with Organizer and wait for processing. In App Store Connect, create the new version entry, paste release notes, and attach the build. Add the build to the correct phased release setting, then submit for review. After approval, flip the release to manual and coordinate the go-live in Slack. Checklist items to confirm: marketing version matches tag, screenshots are current, and crash-free sessions are stable before releasing.

### Adding a new REST endpoint to our Swift client 1) Create a new `Endpoint` case in the API enum with path, method, and auth requirement. 2) Define request/response models (Codable) and map server keys with CodingKeys. 3) Add a client function that calls the endpoint and returns the decoded model. 4) Write a unit test using the stubbed transport: assert request URL, HTTP method, headers, and body. 5) Update the public protocol so callers can access it, and add a short usage snippet to the README. Notes: keep date parsing consistent with the existing ISO8601 formatter; if the endpoint returns an envelope, decode the envelope first and expose the inner payload.

### Debugging a flaky CI failure (SwiftPM) If CI fails only on the build step, reproduce locally with the same command and clean state. First run `swift package clean`, then delete DerivedData for the workspace. Re-run tests with verbose output to capture the failing target and the exact compiler invocation. If the error is missing symbols, check for conditional compilation flags and ensure the product is linked in the test target. If it’s a timeout, increase the test timeout and isolate the slow suite by running it alone. Once fixed, push a minimal commit and re-run the pipeline. Keep logs: paste the failing command and the first error line into the ticket.

### Rotating API keys and deploying backend Procedure for key rotation: - Generate the new key in the provider console and label it with today’s date. - Update the secret in the deployment environment (staging first), then run smoke tests. - Deploy staging and verify the health endpoint plus one real request path. - Promote the same secret to production and deploy. - After production is stable for 30 minutes, revoke the old key. Operational notes: always `deploy staging and verify`, and never revoke first. If errors spike, roll back immediately and restore the previous secret value.

### Running database migrations safely Before applying migrations, create a backup snapshot and confirm you can restore it. Put the app into maintenance mode, then run migrations on a single primary node. Watch the migration output for locks; if a statement blocks, stop and assess indexes before retrying. After migrations finish, run a quick set of read/write checks and bring the app back online. Finally, monitor error rates and latency for at least 15 minutes. Command order we follow: `backup snapshot`, `maintenance mode`, `run migrations on a single primary node`, then verify.
required_spans: `run migrations on a single primary node`

text: ### Incident response: user login outage Steps during a login outage: 1) Declare the incident in the on-call channel and assign an incident commander. 2) Confirm scope by checking dashboards and synthetic checks; note start time. 3) `disable the feature flag` tied to the recent auth rollout. 4) If recovery doesn’t happen within 5 minutes, roll back the last deploy. 5) Communicate status every 10 minutes, even if unchanged. 6) After recovery, capture a timeline, write the postmortem, and add action items with owners. Reminder: do not chase edge cases mid-incident; stabilize first, then investigate root cause.

## Decision: Token Refresh Concurrency We changed the auth layer so that only one refresh happens at a time per user session. Previously, concurrent API calls would all race to refresh and could overwrite each other’s tokens. ### What we decided - Use a shared `actor` to serialize refresh requests and coalesce waiters. - Cache the in-flight refresh `Task` and reuse it for followers. ### Why This eliminates duplicate refresh calls, reduces 401 storms, and keeps the token source of truth consistent. ### Notes If refresh fails, all awaiters receive the same error and the cached task is cleared. We also added a short jitter before retry to avoid thundering herds after network restoration.

## Decision: Local Storage for Drafts We decided to store unsent message drafts locally so users don’t lose work when the app is backgrounded or the network drops. ### Implementation notes - Persist drafts in `SQLite` with a TTL column. - Key drafts by `conversationId` + `userId`. - Apply a 7-day TTL cleanup on app launch and on successful send. ### Rationale Drafts are user-authored, frequently edited, and must survive crashes. We explicitly avoided syncing drafts to the server because it complicates privacy expectations and increases backend churn. ### Edge cases If a conversation is deleted, drafts for that conversation are purged during the next cleanup run.

## Decision: Analytics Event Naming We standardized analytics event names and properties to reduce downstream query complexity and prevent silent schema drift. ### Convention - Event names use `snake_case` and start with the feature area. - Properties use stable IDs, not UI strings. ### Examples - `checkout_payment_submit` - `search_result_tap` ### Why We previously had multiple names for the same action, and UI text changes broke dashboards. This decision makes events predictable, allows consistent funnel definitions, and simplifies QA validation. ### Guardrails Add new events only via the `AnalyticsEvent` enum to enforce naming and required properties at compile time.

## Decision: Image Cache Policy We updated the image caching strategy to improve scrolling performance without blowing up disk usage. ### Policy - Memory cache: LRU capped at 40 MB. - Disk cache: LRU capped at 250 MB with background trimming. - Respect `Cache-Control` when present, otherwise default to 24 hours. ### Why The previous “cache forever” approach caused large disk growth on power users and made support debugging harder. The new policy keeps common avatars and feed thumbnails hot while ensuring old items are evicted. ### Operational note Disk trimming runs when the app enters background and after large downloads complete.

text: ## Decision: Push Notification Routing We decided to route push notifications using a single deep-link parser so navigation remains consistent across app states. ### Details - All pushes include a `deeplink` field. - The app uses `DeepLinkRouter.parse()` to map the link to a destination. - If parsing fails, we fall back to the notifications inbox. ### Why We previously had separate code paths for cold start vs foreground, causing mismatched screens and hard-to-reproduce bugs. Centralizing routing reduces duplication and allows us to test navigation logic with unit tests. ### Testing Add fixtures for each push type and assert the resulting destination.

## Decision: Feature Flags Ownership We clarified who owns feature flags and how they are retired to reduce long-lived dead code. ### Ownership model - Every flag must have an `owner` and an explicit `sunset_date`. - Flags must be removed within two weeks after rollout reaches 100%. ### Why Flags tended to accumulate, making the codebase harder to reason about and increasing test matrix complexity. This policy makes accountability visible and keeps the system lean. ### Workflow Add flags via the `FeatureFlagRegistry`, and open a removal ticket when the flag is created. Review outstanding sunsets weekly during engineering sync.

## Decision: Error Messaging for Payments We chose to map payment failures to user-facing messages based on error category rather than raw provider codes. ### Mapping - Network/timeouts → “Check your connection and try again.” - Insufficient funds → “Your bank declined this charge.” - Suspected fraud → “Contact your bank to approve the payment.” ### Why Provider codes vary across PSPs and change without notice. Categorizing errors stabilizes UX copy, reduces translation churn, and lets support troubleshoot with consistent internal labels. ### Engineering note We log the original provider code under `psp_code` for debugging, but never display it to the user.

## Decision: Background Sync Cadence We decided to run background sync at a predictable cadence to balance freshness with battery impact. ### Cadence - Use `BGAppRefreshTask` with a 6-hour earliest begin date. - Trigger an immediate sync on app foreground if last sync is older than 30 minutes. ### Why The prior “sync on every launch” approach caused unnecessary network usage and made behavior unpredictable for QA. The new cadence is easier to reason about, improves battery life, and still keeps data reasonably fresh. ### Telemetry Track sync duration, bytes transferred, and failure rate tagged by network type.

### Team preferences (semantic) In this repo, we follow a consistent API style: prefer async/await with explicit `throws` over callback-based completion handlers, and avoid global singletons for dependency management. When adding new network requests, keep Endpoint names in UpperCamelCase and place them next to related models. For decoding, use `JSONDecoder` with `.convertFromSnakeCase` by default unless the API explicitly violates it. For unit tests, use small fixtures and prefer snapshot-style assertions only for stable, deterministic outputs. This note is meant as shared project knowledge, not a task list.

### Build & tooling conventions (semantic) We treat CI as the source of truth: the main branch must stay green, and local scripts should mirror CI steps as closely as possible. Use `swift-format` for formatting; don’t hand-align whitespace. Lint failures should be fixed in the same PR as the change that introduced them. If you need to suppress a lint rule, document why in the nearest comment and keep the scope minimal. When measuring performance, use release configuration and capture results on the same device to reduce variance. This describes the team’s long-term workflow expectations.

### Data modeling guidelines (semantic) Model types should be boring and predictable. Prefer value types for immutable domain concepts and keep reference types for shared, mutable state only when strictly needed. For identifiers, use strongly-typed wrappers (e.g., `UserID`) instead of raw strings. For date handling, store as `Date` internally and format only at presentation boundaries. When mapping from API DTOs into domain models, perform validation in the mapper layer and make invalid states unrepresentable. This is a durable rule set for how we structure data across the app.

### UI architecture notes (semantic) We use a unidirectional flow for user interactions: Views emit actions, a reducer produces new state, and side effects live in explicit services. Avoid letting view controllers own business logic. Instead, keep state transformations in testable units and inject dependencies via initializers. Navigation should be driven from state where feasible so deep links and back behavior remain consistent. These notes capture architectural decisions that apply across features, not a one-off implementation detail.

### Logging & privacy policy (semantic) All telemetry must minimize user data exposure. Never log raw access tokens, email addresses, or full request/response bodies. When debugging, prefer redacted summaries and opt-in local logging. Use structured logs with stable keys so we can aggregate events without parsing free-form strings. Keep log levels consistent: info for lifecycle milestones, debug for verbose traces, error for failures that require attention. If a field might contain PII, treat it as sensitive by default. This is enduring team knowledge about safe logging behavior.

### Git workflow expectations (semantic) We squash-merge PRs to keep history readable. Branch names should be descriptive and include a short ticket reference when available. Write commit messages in imperative mood. Before requesting review, rebase on the latest main and resolve conflicts locally. Avoid force-pushing after review begins unless you coordinate with reviewers. PR descriptions should include a brief risk assessment and a rollback plan when changes affect critical paths. This is shared guidance for how we collaborate in git over time.

### Error handling philosophy (semantic) We distinguish between user-recoverable errors and developer-actionable failures. User-recoverable cases should surface friendly messages with clear next steps; developer-actionable failures should capture context for investigation. Prefer typed error enums over stringly-typed errors. When bridging errors across layers, preserve the original error as an underlying cause. Do not swallow errors silently; if you intentionally ignore one, record the rationale. This is a long-lived principle for consistent error behavior across modules.

### Caching rules (semantic) Cache only what is safe to reuse and define a single authority for cache invalidation. Prefer time-based expiration for volatile data and version-based invalidation for schema changes. Keys must be deterministic and include all parameters that affect the response. For disk caches, store metadata (createdAt, ttl, and size) alongside payloads so eviction policies can be enforced. If cached data influences UI, always display a freshness indicator when content may be stale. This records stable caching norms for the project.

### Project Decision We’re standardizing on a single dependency injection approach for the app to reduce onboarding time. - Use `Swinject` for runtime DI. - Avoid mixing constructor injection with ad-hoc singletons. - Container lives in `AppCompositionRoot` and is the only place allowed to register services. Rationale: SwiftUI previews and test targets need the same graph with small overrides. This decision replaces the earlier approach where feature modules created their own containers. Follow-up: - Add a lint rule to block new global singletons. - Document how to inject `NetworkClient` into repositories. Note: this does not change the API surface; it’s an internal wiring policy.

### Architecture Note: Caching Strategy We will cache user profile data to improve perceived performance on cold start. - Cache is keyed by `userId` and stored in `UserDefaults` as a small JSON blob. - TTL is 12 hours; refresh happens opportunistically after app launch. - If decoding fails, drop cache and fetch from network. Rationale: profiles are read frequently across tabs and rarely change. We explicitly do not cache large collections here. Implementation details: - Repository exposes `getProfile(forceRefresh:)`. - Use `ISO8601DateFormatter` for stored timestamps. This is a semantic rule about what we cache and why, not a log of a single event.

### Build & Tooling Decision To keep CI consistent, we will pin the toolchain and enforce formatting. - Xcode version is pinned to `Xcode 15.2` on CI runners. - Swift formatting is enforced via `swift-format` in a pre-commit hook and in CI. - The formatting configuration lives at `./.swift-format`. Rationale: contributors use different local setups; pinning reduces “works on my machine” diffs and noisy PRs. Notes: - CI should fail if formatting changes are required. - Local developers can run `make format` to apply fixes. This is an enduring project convention, not a one-off instruction.

### Security Policy: Secrets Handling We must not ship or log sensitive tokens in the app. - API keys are loaded from `Keychain` at runtime. - Never print auth headers; redact with `***REDACTED***`. - Crash reports must not include user identifiers. Rationale: logs are routinely attached to support tickets and can be shared externally. This policy applies to all modules, including debug builds. Enforcement: - Add a unit test around the logger formatter. - Add a code review checklist item: “No secrets in strings.” This is a semantic security rule meant to be remembered long-term.

### Data Model Convention All persistent models must use stable identifiers and explicit versioning. - Every stored entity has an `id: UUID` and a `schemaVersion` integer. - Migrations are handled in a single place: `PersistenceMigrator`. - When adding fields, provide defaults for old data. Rationale: we’ve had crashes from decoding older payloads after upgrades. Versioning makes migrations predictable and testable. Testing: - Add fixtures for schemaVersion 1 and 2. - Validate that decoding older fixtures succeeds. This is a project-wide semantic convention, not tied to a specific incident.

### UX Guideline: Loading States We will use a consistent pattern for content loading across screens. - Always show skeletons, not spinners, for first-load lists. - Skeleton duration is capped at 800ms; if data arrives sooner, still show a short fade. - Empty states must include an action, not just text. Implementation: - Use `SkeletonView` for list placeholders. - Apply `easeInOut(duration: 0.2)` for the transition. Rationale: users perceive skeletons as faster and they reduce layout jumps. This is a standing UX rule for future features.

### API Design Rule: Error Mapping Networking errors must map into a small, user-facing domain. - Transport failures map to `AppError.networkUnavailable`. - HTTP 401 maps to `AppError.sessionExpired`. - Unknown server responses map to `AppError.unexpected`. Rationale: UI should not branch on raw status codes; it should react to stable error cases. This also helps analytics: we can count error categories rather than exploding the taxonomy. Implementation notes: - Centralize mapping in the API client layer. - Keep the mapping deterministic and covered by unit tests. This is a semantic contract for the codebase.

### Release Process Convention We will follow a predictable versioning and branching strategy. - Use `Semantic Versioning` for marketing versions. - Release branches are named `release/x.y.z`. - Hotfixes must be cherry-picked back to `main` and `develop`. Rationale: the team needs a shared mental model for what a release contains and how patches flow. This reduces confusion during urgent fixes and makes changelogs reliable. Checklist snippet: - Update release notes. - Tag the build after App Store submission. This is a long-term process rule, not a timestamped activity log.

### Decision log — Auth refresh strategy We switched the session model from storing access tokens in the keychain to a short-lived access token plus rotating refresh token held in secure storage. The key reason is reducing blast radius if the app is backgrounded and memory-dumped; refresh tokens are scoped and can be revoked server-side. Implementation note: on `401` responses, the client should attempt a single refresh, then replay the original request with the new access token. Any concurrent requests during refresh should queue behind the same in-flight refresh to avoid a refresh storm. Also: if refresh fails with invalid_grant, force logout and clear local caches. This decision depends on the backend supporting token rotation and a revocation endpoint.

text: ### Architecture note — Why we chose event-sourcing for drafts We decided to model user-created drafts as a stream of immutable events rather than mutable records. This is to support cross-device merge without relying on last-write-wins, and to allow replay for debugging corrupted draft state. Events are compact, append-only, and can be periodically squashed into snapshots. Important constraint: the UI should never depend on server round-trips to render the latest local draft. Instead, it rehydrates from the local event log and then syncs asynchronously. We are explicitly avoiding per-field conflict resolution at the API layer; merging happens via deterministic reducers in the client.

### Product reasoning — Notifications “quiet hours” defaults We agreed that new installs should start with quiet hours enabled by default, because our target users are caregivers and night interruptions have a disproportionate negative impact on retention. Quiet hours still allow critical alerts, but suppress non-urgent reminders. Copy guidelines: the settings screen should explain that users can override this, and it should present the default window as 10pm–7am in the device’s locale, editable in 15-minute increments. Also, when users first enable notifications, show a short confirmation explaining what will still get through. This is a behavioral baseline we can A/B test later, but we need a sensible, humane default.

### Design rationale — Search ranking for the “People” screen We settled on ranking results primarily by recent interaction rather than alphabetical order. The main goal is reducing time-to-contact for users who repeatedly message the same few people. The algorithm uses a decay-weighted score based on message sends, calls initiated, and profile opens. Edge case: a newly added contact with no interactions should still be discoverable, so exact prefix matches get a temporary boost. Also, we decided to never use “last online” as a ranking signal to avoid privacy creep. This aligns with the app’s promise of being helpful without being intrusive.
required_spans: ranking results primarily by recent interaction

text: ### Engineering note — Offline-first sync conflict policy We documented that the app is offline-first and must tolerate long disconnections. For sync conflicts, we chose “client wins for drafts” and “server wins for published items.” Drafts are personal and should preserve the user’s local intent; published items represent shared truth. To make this reliable, drafts carry a client-generated `draftRevision` monotonic counter, and published items carry server `etag` values. When reconciling, drafts merge by applying local deltas atop the last acknowledged server snapshot; published items require re-fetch and user-visible resolution if the `etag` changed. This policy is deliberately asymmetric and should be consistent across platforms.

### Team agreement — Error handling UX for failed uploads We decided that failed uploads should surface as a persistent banner with a clear retry action, rather than transient toasts. The banner should list the count of pending items, and tapping it opens a queue view with per-item status. Key detail: retries should use exponential backoff with jitter, but user-initiated retries bypass backoff and attempt immediately. Also, we agreed that cancelling an upload should not delete the local file; it only removes it from the upload queue. This is about maintaining trust: users need to know their work isn’t lost and that the app is actively trying to recover.

text: ## Team sync (Mon) Met with **Rina Patel** and **Jamal Ortiz** after standup to align on how we handle cross-team communication. Rina prefers a quick DM first for sensitive topics, then a short public summary in #eng-updates. Jamal asked that we avoid surprise pings during his focus block and instead schedule a 10-minute slot. text: ### Project: Collab iOS We’re using `Memory.swift` to store *team-specific* preferences. In the current sprint, I’m pairing with Zac on the networking refactor and want the memory system to remember what *this* repo expects. Notes: - Prefer `async/await` APIs in new endpoints. - When stubbing network calls, keep the stub JSON in `Tests/Fixtures/`. - Naming: feature modules use `CollabFeatureKit` suffix. - For PRs, link the ticket in the first line and include a short rollout note. Reminder: Zac asked that I don’t add third-party persistence; keep everything in-memory unless the team approves otherwise.

### Repo notes (Collab) This project has a specific convention for how we document decisions. When you summarize changes, add a short section titled **Rationale** followed by 2–3 bullets. This is important because the team reads PR descriptions more than commit messages. Local workflow reminders: - Run `xcodebuild test` only for the impacted scheme; don’t blanket-run everything. - If a build fails on CI, check the `DerivedData` paths in logs first. - Keep warnings at zero; treat warnings as errors. Zac’s preference: use `rg` for code search instead of `grep` so results are faster and consistent across machines.

### Memory system integration notes In this repo, the memory layer is meant to capture *project-local* norms rather than general best practices. For Collab, the key is that user-facing strings must go through localization, even for internal tools. Implementation constraints: - Add new strings to `Localizable.strings` and keep keys stable. - Avoid hardcoding copy in view models. - When adding analytics, route events through `AnalyticsClient` rather than calling the vendor SDK directly. Also: the team wants consistent error surfaces—wrap network failures into `AppError.network` so UI tests can assert predictable messages.

### Collab build + tooling conventions When editing Swift packages or project settings, the team wants changes to be reproducible. Don’t hand-edit the `.xcodeproj` unless the change is tiny; prefer scripted or deterministic edits. Quick checklist: - If you add files, confirm target membership and group placement. - Keep SwiftFormat disabled for now; CI doesn’t run it. - If you need to add a package, document it in `Docs/Dependencies.md`. One more repo-specific rule: all feature flags must live behind `FeatureFlagService` so we can toggle them per environment without recompiling.

### Testing practices (Collab) This codebase treats tests as part of the public contract. When you change behavior, update the snapshot or the unit test in the same PR—don’t defer. Repo-specific reminders: - UI tests rely on `AccessibilityIdentifiers.swift`; add new IDs there. - For async tests, the team standard is `XCTestExpectation` with a 2s timeout. - Keep mocks simple; prefer fakes over dynamic stubbing frameworks. If a test needs a fixed date, use `FixedClock()` instead of `Date()` so CI runs are deterministic.

### API client notes for this repo The Collab app’s API layer has a house style that the team cares about. When you add or adjust endpoints, keep request construction centralized and avoid sprinkling URL composition throughout features. Conventions: - Base URL comes from `Environment.current.apiBaseURL`. - Add headers through `RequestSigner` (don’t set auth headers manually). - Decode responses using `JSONDecoder.collabConfigured` to ensure date strategy matches backend. Also, Zac explicitly asked that we keep retries off by default; only enable retries per-endpoint when product requests it.

### Logging + diagnostics (Collab) For this project, logs are meant to be actionable in QA builds but minimal in production. Don’t use `print()` for debugging; it pollutes the console and doesn’t integrate with our log collection. Rules of thumb: - Use `Logger.collab` with categories like `network`, `ui`, and `storage`. - Never log PII; redact emails and tokens. - For critical paths, add a signpost via `OSSignpost` so we can measure performance regressions. If you add new logs, ensure they’re behind `BuildConfig.isDebug` where appropriate.

### Release process notes (Collab) This repo has a specific release checklist that the team expects contributors to follow. When preparing a release candidate, update the changelog and verify versioning is correct. Project-specific steps: - Bump `CFBundleShortVersionString` and `CFBundleVersion` together. - Update `CHANGELOG.md` with user-facing bullet points. - Confirm the build uses the `Release` configuration and not a debug profile. ## Security: Redwood SSO We’re rolling out SAML SSO for enterprise customers. The main blocker is handling certificate rotation without downtime. The service should fetch IdP metadata daily and cache it, but also allow manual refresh for urgent rotations. Requirements: - Support multiple signing certificates per IdP. - Validate `NotBefore`/`NotOnOrAfter` strictly. - Store metadata version history for debugging. Implementation note: - Add an endpoint `POST /sso/idp/:id/refresh-metadata` guarded by admin role. Operational guidance: - If validation fails, fall back to the last known good metadata. - Alert on consecutive failures.

It reproduces most often after the app returns from background and the user quickly navigates to the detail screen. Hypothesis: - The action is wired to a stale view model instance. Fix proposal: - Move the handler into the screen’s coordinator. - Ensure the UI always binds to the latest `DetailViewModel`. Test plan: - Add an automated UI test named `testShareButtonAfterBackground`. - Verify the share sheet appears within 2 seconds. ## Infra: Orion Kubernetes We’re tuning autoscaling for the API deployment. Right now, HPA reacts too slowly to traffic spikes and we see elevated p95 latency. The plan is to scale on both CPU and request rate via custom metrics. Changes to make: - Set `minReplicas: 6` to reduce cold-start impact. - Configure `targetAverageUtilization: 55` for CPU. - Add a Prometheus metric for `http_requests_per_second` and wire it to HPA. Runbook updates: - Document how to validate scaling during load tests. - Add alerts when replicas hit max. Constraint: avoid overprovisioning; keep baseline cost within budget.

### Collab iOS app – onboarding refactor (Feb 14) I’m changing onboarding because users keep missing the invite step. In `OnboardingFlowCoordinator`, keep all routing decisions inside the coordinator; the view models should only emit events. Also, the new invite screen must be reachable from the welcome screen and from the post-signup confirmation. Implementation notes: - Add `InviteCodeViewController` to the flow after the email verification screen. - Use the existing `Analytics.track("invite_code_opened")` event when the screen appears. - Persist the last entered code in `UserDefaults` under `invite.lastCode` so a back navigation doesn’t wipe it. Reminder: keep the UI copy consistent with “Enter your team code”.

### Networking cleanup – profile endpoint parity While aligning Android and iOS APIs, ensure our Swift client matches the backend contract for the profile update call. In `APIClient`, the request must be PATCH and the body should be JSON with snake_case keys. Don’t reuse the old `updateUserProfile` that still sends multipart. Details to keep: - Endpoint path is `/v1/profile` and requires the `Authorization` header. - Expected response includes `profile_version` so we can invalidate cached views. - Update `ProfileService` to return `Result<ProfileDTO, APIError>` and map 409 into a typed conflict. Add a note in the changelog that `ProfileDTO.profileVersion` is now required for cache invalidation.

### Local persistence – message drafts Users want drafts preserved per conversation. Implement draft storage keyed by thread id, but keep it scoped to the current account to avoid leaking drafts after logout. Use `DraftStore` as the only access point; views should not touch storage directly. Plan: - Store drafts in a small SQLite table with columns `account_id`, `thread_id`, and `text`. - When `ChatViewController` disappears, call `DraftStore.saveDraft(threadId:text:)`. - When opening a thread, call `DraftStore.loadDraft(threadId:)` and prefill the composer. Edge cases: - Clearing the composer should delete the row. - Logout should trigger `DraftStore.purgeForAccount(accountId:)`. Keep the feature behind the `drafts_enabled` remote flag for now.

### Release checklist – push notification deep links For the next build, verify deep links from push notifications route correctly. The key requirement: tapping a notification with a thread id must open that thread even if the app is cold-started. Implementation reminders: - Parse `notification_payload.thread_id` in the push handler. - Route via `DeepLinkRouter.handle(url:)` so analytics and auth gating stay consistent. - If the user is logged out, save the pending link in `PendingDeepLinkStore` and replay after login. QA steps: 1) Kill the app, send a push for an existing thread. 2) Tap the push; confirm the correct chat opens and the unread badge updates. Do not add any new universal links in this release; only notification routing.

### UI theming – dark mode contrast fix Design reported unread message badges are too dim in dark mode. Update the theme tokens rather than hardcoding colors. In `ThemePalette`, adjust the semantic `badgeUnreadBackground` to meet contrast while keeping the rest of the palette stable. Notes: - The badge is rendered by `UnreadBadgeView` and should reference the semantic token only. - The desired dark value is `#3B82F6` and light mode remains unchanged. - Verify the badge still looks correct in the “mentions” list and the thread list. Acceptance: - No direct `UIColor(red:...)` calls inside `UnreadBadgeView`. - Snapshot tests for dark mode should be updated only if the badge pixel values change. Keep the change minimal and don’t touch typography.

### Crash fix – audio playback interruption We’re seeing crashes when resuming audio after an interruption (phone call, Siri). The stack points to stale observers. Fix by centralizing session handling in `AudioSessionManager` and removing duplicate notifications. Requirements: - Register for `AVAudioSession.interruptionNotification` once, inside `AudioSessionManager.start()`. - Ensure `PlayerViewModel` doesn’t add its own interruption observer anymore. - On interruption ended, re-activate the session and call `AudioSessionManager.resumeIfNeeded()` before trying to play. Regression checks: - Start playback, trigger interruption, then resume. - Background the app during interruption and return. Do not change the audio engine implementation; only observer lifecycle and session reactivation.
